hero.title: Places she'd take her own kids.
hero.lead: A day out with Eunjung isn't a tour — it's her showing you the corner of Korea she loves most, at her own pace. She speaks gentle English, can drive you herself, and always knows where to eat.

paragraphs: Nothing on this page is scripted. There is no fixed route and no set of must-see stops. Eunjung plans the day around what you're curious about, what the weather is doing, and what happens to be in season that week.

She can take you deep into Seoul's side streets — the bookbinder she buys paper from, the tea house her mother used to visit — or an hour outside the city to a pottery village or a quiet hill where nobody speaks English and the food is better for it.
She'll pick you up in her car or meet you at Jeongja Station. Lunch somewhere she trusts. Back home by dusk, or later if you're having too much fun to leave.

places.name: Ikseon-dong tea alleys
places.blurb: A warren of narrow hanok alleys that have turned into tea houses and tiny shops. Eunjung knows which ones are worth it and which are Instagram traps.

places.name: Bukchon stone steps
places.blurb: Not the postcard street. The quiet side of Bukchon, where the rooflines are still crooked and somebody's grandmother waves from a courtyard.

places.name: Seochon hidden bookshop
places.blurb: A second-floor used bookshop that smells like paper and pine. Tea downstairs after, at a place with four seats and a cat.

places.name: Gwangjang market, the back row
places.blurb: Past the bindaetteok tourists line up for, there's an aunt who's been making mayak gimbap since 1982. Eunjung will introduce you.

places.name: Jongmyo shrine, near closing
places.blurb: Slip in an hour before it empties out. The oldest Confucian shrine still in use, cool stone under old pines, almost no one around.

places.name: A pottery village in Yeoju
places.blurb: Two hours east. The village where Eunjung buys her own ceramics. Lunch at a place with no English menu and a wood stove.

places.name: Gapyeong countryside
places.blurb: Rice paddies, pine hills, a tiny roadside pajeon place she stops at every time. Good for an autumn day.

places.name: Tancheon at sunrise
places.blurb: If you're staying with her: wake up early, cross the road, walk along the stream as the herons fish. Ends in a bakery she likes.

howItWorks.title: Tell Eunjung about your trip
howItWorks.body: A few lines about when you're in Korea, how long you have, and what you're curious about. She replies within a day or two.

howItWorks.title: She designs a custom day
howItWorks.body: Based on the weather, the season, and what's open. She'll send a rough plan and you can add, cut, or swap anything.

howItWorks.title: Spend the day with your Korean mom
howItWorks.body: She picks you up or meets you at Jeongja Station. Lunch is on her. Home by dusk, or later — whatever the day asks for.

sampleItineraries.title: A day in Seoul's quiet corners
sampleItineraries.body: Meet at Anguk Station. Slow loop through Bukchon's back alleys, a stop at a traditional bookbinder, tea where her mother used to go. Lunch at a place with hand-cut noodles. Jongmyo shrine just before it closes. Back home by early evening.

sampleItineraries.title: Outside the city — nature and local food
sampleItineraries.body: Eunjung drives. Two hours out to a pottery village in Yeoju, a kiln visit, lunch with the owner. Afternoon walk in the hills around Gwangju. Home the long way, through rice paddies at golden hour.
